todaysEvents.summary: Iran's new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei broke his silence, vowing the Strait of Hormuz will remain blocked and attacks on Gulf shipping will continue — removing any near-term normalisation case. (Mar 13)

Iranian President Pezeshkian set ceasefire terms requiring US/Israel recognition of Iran's rights and payment of reparations; analysts noted a possible off-ramp but Washington and Jerusalem rejected the conditions as a non-starter. (Mar 12)
Six vessels were struck in the Persian Gulf on Mar 12, including a Thai cargo ship set ablaze in Hormuz; Iran warned oil could reach $200/bbl if the Gulf-wide closure extends. (Mar 12)

scenarios.label: Base
scenarios.description: The March 11 reserve-release package caps the left tail of immediate shortages, and a bilateral back-channel still exists, but maritime security remains broken. Hormuz disruption lasts another 2-4 weeks before escorted traffic normalises. Brent trades mostly $85-100/bbl. Basra exports recover and Saudi/UAE spare capacity offsets part of the Iraq/Kuwait shortfall. ASEAN LNG buyers rely on Australian and US spot cargoes; P&I cover returns only after an enforceable transit regime is in place.

scenarios.label: Stress
scenarios.description: Maritime disruption persists through April. Repeated attacks on merchant shipping and the spread of incidents into Iraqi waters keep insurers sidelined and escorted traffic thin. Brent oscillates in a $98-120/bbl band even with IEA/SPR releases. Basra and Gulf loadings remain intermittent, Qatar restart stays partial, and JKM/TTF remain elevated. ASEAN current-account pressure rebuilds; Bank Indonesia, BSP, and regional fuel-subsidy authorities stay on intervention watch.

scenarios.label: Tail
scenarios.description: A wider Gulf war shuts both Hormuz and Basra export flows for months and spreads attacks into the western Indian Ocean. Oil reaches $150-180/bbl despite reserve releases. LNG becomes scarce for Asian spot buyers, Qatar full restart fails, and ASEAN recession risk surges for Thailand and the Philippines. P&I cover does not return for multiple quarters and trade-finance risk becomes systemic.
